content.full_text:
1 7 












Ms. Sara Cameron 
Fair political Practices commission 
1100 
K St. 
Sacramento, Ca. 
95814 
Dear Ms. Cameron, 
P. O. Box 434 
Cambria, Ca. 
93428 
July 20, 1979 
Based upon a telephone conversation I had with you on 
June 20, 1979, I 
am sending you this letter regarding the 
conditions of conflict of interest re: my May appointment 
to the South Central Coast Regional Commission. 
Prior to 
my appointment to the Commission I had been active in a number 
of organizations related to my field of agriculture. 
I would 
appreciate it if you would be able to send me a letter stating 
the opinion and position of the FPPC regarding my continuation 
In these organizations and activities. 
I currently hold the position of San Luis Obispo County 
Farm Bureau Treasurer (an office which will expire in October 
to be replaced by a term as County Secretary). 
This is an 
unpaid position, where I actually do not handle any funds, 
but sit on the county executive committee and the board of 
directors for the county. 
For the last 3 years I have been appointed to the position 
of San Luis Obispo County Cowbelle Legislative Chairman and 
commencing in 1978 San Luis Obispo County Cattlemen's Legislative 
Alert Chairman. 
These two positions are inter-related. 
The 
are unpaid positions. 
commencing at my appointment to the 
Coastal Commission I have not been re-imbursed for any of 
the activities I have undertaken in these two chairmanships, 
and do not plan to handle any moneys in these positions in 
the future. 
The organizations will be furnishing my materials 
needed for mailing out legislative letters under the direction 
of the organizations. 
My main activity as Legislative Chairman 
is to keep both organizations updated on local, state and 
national legislative issues and to mail the county memberships 
letters requesting action as I receive directives from the 
state or county organizations. 
I sit on the boards of both 
organizations 
this capacity. 


Sara Cameron 
-2-
Based upon the information I am able to give you, I 
hope that you will be able to answer the following questions 
that have surfaced since my Coastal appointment. 
1. 
It it permissable to remain in the capacity of 
Treasurer and to continue on to Secretary of the San Luis 
Obispo County Farm Bureau, and to remain as Legislative 
Chairman for the San Luis Obispo County Cowbelles and 
Cattlemen? 
2. 
What is the conflict if a Coastal related issue 
arises in these organizations and how should such an issue 
be handled (especially as legislative chairman)? 
3. 
Can I as a private citizen voice my opinions regarding 
Coastal matters, both in these organizations and as L give 
speeches and in talking to individuals? 
Where does the liability 
lie or extend? 
4. 
Based upon my appointment to the Commission, can 
I as an individual support issues and candidates of local, 
state, and national importance and what is the advisability 
of entering into such activities and what are the liabilities? 
5. 
What is, if any, the conflict of 
Coastal Act, and laws as they are written? 
public 
as to what the law reads wither in 
newspapers)? 
Where would the liability be 
discussing the 
(ie. informing th 
person or by the 
? 
6. 
As I belong to a family partnership (informal as 
no partnership papers have ever been drawn up) with my parents 
(partners) owning land in the coastal zone that we operate 
(approximately 32 acres of a 2000 acre operation), I need to 
know where my conflict of interest exists when voting on 
coastal agricultural issues. 
Does it apply to issues on 
neighboring property, or does it extend to any agricultural 
operation in the Coastal Zone regardless of the location? 
Does the similarity of the operation in the zone have a 
relavance or would a conflict exist regardless of the similarity 
of the operations (ie. any other beef operation -
even though 
our business is only in the cow-calf production)? 
7. 
Because I own preferred and common stock in three 
(3) Standard Oil Companies, I realize that I will be unable 
to vote on issues these three companies bring before the 
Commission, but will this stock ownership create a conflict 
of interest when I vote on issues from other oil companies? 
(ie. other Standard Oil Companies or non-Standard Oil Companies?) 


Sara Cameron 
-3-
I do need the answers to each of these questions as 
they are of vital importance to the organizations I belong 
to and to my ability to function as a Commissioner. 
I 
would appreciate it if you could send these answers and 
statement to me by August 15, 1979. 
The organizations are 
now forming their 1980 nominations for their offerships and 
need the answers to all of these problems. 
~nk~~~~ 
Joy Anne Fitzhugh 
\~

sections.question: 1. It it permissable to remain in the capacity of Treasurer and to continue on to Secretary of the San Luis Obispo County Farm Bureau, and to remain as Legislative Chairman for the San Luis Obispo County Cowbelles and Cattlemen? 2. What is the conflict if a Coastal related issue arises in these organizations and how should such an issue be handled (especially as legislative chairman)? 3. Can I as a private citizen voice my opinions regarding Coastal matters, both in these organizations and as L give speeches and in talking to individuals? Where does the liability lie or extend? 4. Based upon my appointment to the Commission, can I as an individual support issues and candidates of local, state, and national importance and what is the advisability of entering into such activities and what are the liabilities? 5. What is, if any, the conflict of public discussing the Coastal Act, and laws as they are written? (ie. informing th person or by newspapers)? Where would the liability be? 6. As I belong to a family partnership (informal as no partnership papers have ever been drawn up) with my parents (partners) owning land in the coastal zone that we operate (approximately 32 acres of a 2000 acre operation), I need to know where my conflict of interest exists when voting on coastal agricultural issues. Does it apply to issues on neighboring property, or does it extend to any agricultural operation in the Coastal Zone regardless of the location? 7. Because I own preferred and common stock in three (3) Standard Oil Companies, I realize that I will be unable to vote on issues these three companies bring before the Commission, but will this stock ownership create a conflict of interest when I vote on issues from other oil companies?
sections.question_synthetic: An appointee to the South Central Coast Regional Commission seeks FPPC guidance on conflict of interest issues related to her continued involvement in agricultural organizations (Farm Bureau, Cowbelles, Cattlemen), her family's land holdings in the coastal zone, her Standard Oil Company stock ownership, and her ability to engage in political activities and voice opinions on Coastal Act matters.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the public to FPPC, not an FPPC response. The letter contains detailed background information followed by seven numbered questions. No CONCLUSION or FPPC response section is present in this document. Letter dated July 20, 1979, requesting response by August 15, 1979.

Based upon my appointment to the Commission, can I as an individual support issues and candidates of local, state, and national importance and what is the advisability of entering into such activities and what are the liabilities? 5. What is, if any, the conflict of public discussing the Coastal Act, and laws as they are written? (ie. informing th person or by newspapers)? Where would the liability be? 6. As I belong to a family partnership (informal as no partnership papers have ever been drawn up) with my parents (partners) owning land in the coastal zone that we operate (approximately 32 acres of a 2000 acre operation), I need to know where my conflict of interest exists when voting on coastal agricultural issues. Does it apply to issues on neighboring property, or does it extend to any agricultural operation in the Coastal Zone regardless of the location? 7. Because I own preferred and common stock in three (3) Standard Oil Companies, I realize that I will be unable to vote on issues these three companies bring before the Commission, but will this stock ownership create a conflict of interest when I vote on issues from other oil companies?

embedding.summary: Joy Anne Fitzhugh, a newly appointed commissioner to the South Central Coast Regional Commission, requests FPPC advice on seven specific conflict of interest questions regarding her continued participation in agricultural organizations, family farm ownership in the coastal zone, stock ownership in Standard Oil companies, and her ability to engage in political activities and public advocacy.
